coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon-based Ag-Pd bimetallic nanocomposite and the application in electroless copper deposition
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Luo
Li, Peipei
Liu, Xiang
Zeng, Wensheng
Zhang, Youyu
Liu, Meiling
Yao, Shouzhuo
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, carbon dots (C-dots) based AgPd/C nanocomposite (AgPd/C NCs) was synthesized and firstly applied as the catalyst in the electroless copper deposition. The hydroxyl-rich C-dots synthesized by one-pot electrochemical carbonization method can reduce Ag+ into Ag nanoparticles to form Ag/carbon nanocomposites (Ag/C NCs), which were then utilized as the template to synthesize AgPd/C NCs that have palladium-rich surface and silver-rich core. The results show that AgPd/C NCs can act as the activator and be used in electroless copper deposition of printed circuit boards (PCBs) with the backlight coefficient of 9.5 with the palladium concentration as low as 7 ppm. The speed in the deposition of copper and the stability of the catalyst are better than those of the traditional Sn/Pd colloidal activator at the same concentration, which promises the AgPd/C NCs good substitutes in electroless copper deposition in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots
AgPd/C nanocomposites
Electroless copper deposition
Activator
Printed circuit board (PCBs)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Ag/C NCs and Pd/C NCs
Preparation and characterization of Ag/C NCs, Pd/C NCs and AgPd/C NCs
Catalytic activity of Pd-based nanomaterials in electroless copper deposition

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21974042 , 21904039 , 21645008 ), the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (18A010), the Science and Technology Department of Hunan Province ( 2021JJ30012 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of AgPd/C NCs and the application in the process of electroless copper deposition.

TEM images of C-dots (A), Ag/C NCs (B), Pd/C NCs (C), AgPd/C NCs (D) and (E) corresponding EDX elemental mapping of Ag and Pd in AgPd/C NCs (E). High-resolution images of C-dots (F), Ag/C NCs (G), Pd/C NCs (H), AgPd/C NCs (I). (J) XPS survey spectra of Pd/C NCs (a) and AgPd/C NCs (b). (K-L) High resolution spectra of C 1s (K) and Pd 3d (L) of Pd/C NCs. (M-O) High resolution spectra of C 1s, Ag 3d and Pd 3d of AgPd/C NCs. (P, Q) Powder XRD pattern of Pd/C NCs (P) and AgPd/C NCs (Q).
(A) UV-vis and infrared (B) spectra of C-dots (a), Pd/C NCs (b), Ag/C NCs (c) and AgPd/C NCs (d). The EDX of Pd/C NCs (C) and AgPd/C NCs (D).

Backlight images observed under the optical microscope after copper plating activated by (A) AgPd/C NCs(1), (B) AgPd/C NCs(2), (C) Pd/C NCs(3) and (D) Sn/Pd (Pd=7 ppm); SEM images of the obtained Cu film activated by conventional Sn/Pd colloid (E) and AgPd/C NCs (F).

(A) Zeta potentials of Sn/Pd, C-dots, Pd/C NCs and AgPd/C NCs. (B) Variation of copper deposition activated by AgPd/C NCs(2) with time during the copper plating. (C) EIS of C-dots (a), Ag/C NCs (b) and AgPd/C NCs (c).

LSV curves of electroless copper bath catalyzed by AgPd/C NCs(1) (a), AgPd/C NCs(2) (b), Sn/Pd (Pd=10 ppm) (c) and Sn/Pd (Pd=7 ppm) (d) during copper ion reduction in the cathodic bath (A) and HCHO oxidation in the anodic reaction (B). (C) The LSV curves of AgPd/C NCs(1) (a) and AgPd/C NCs(2) (b) stored for one month, and the newly made Sn/Pd (Pd=10 ppm) (c) and Sn/Pd (Pd=7 ppm) (d). (D) Anodic and cathodic polarization curves of AgPd/C NCs(1) (A, a) and AgPd/C NCs(2) (B, b) as activators of electroless copper deposition.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon-based Ag-Pd bimetallic nanocomposite and the application in electroless copper deposition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education, China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education, China)
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aKey Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Traditional Chinese Medicine Research (Ministry of Education, China), College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan Normal University, Changsha 410081, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Huizhou Jinsheng New Electronic Technology Co., Ltd., Huizhou 516000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huizhou Jinsheng New Electronic Technology Co.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bHuizhou Jinsheng New Electronic Technology Co., Ltd., Huizhou 516000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, carbon dots (C-dots) based AgPd/C nanocomposite (AgPd/C NCs) was synthesized and firstly applied as the catalyst in the electroless copper deposition. The hydroxyl-rich C-dots synthesized by one-pot electrochemical carbonization method can reduce Ag+ into Ag nanoparticles to form Ag/carbon nanocomposites (Ag/C NCs), which were then utilized as the template to synthesize AgPd/C NCs that have palladium-rich surface and silver-rich core. The results show that AgPd/C NCs can act as the activator and be used in electroless copper deposition of printed circuit boards (PCBs) with the backlight coefficient of 9.5 with the palladium concentration as low as 7 ppm. The speed in the deposition of copper and the stability of the catalyst are better than those of the traditional Sn/Pd colloidal activator at the same concentration, which promises the AgPd/C NCs good substitutes in electroless copper deposition in the future.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Electroless copper deposition (ECD) is an important technology in advanced nanocircuit industries, which can be catalyzed by noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) [1]. ECD can be carried out on any non-conductive substrate, which has been extensively employed in printed circuit board (PCB) manufacturing. The most widely used field is the hole metallization, which is used to complete the connection of wires between layers of the double-sided or multilayer printed boards. A series of steps are included in the electroless copper plating, and the activation step is the most important one. In the activation step, the colloidal catalyst is adsorbed on the insulating substrate and acts as an electron carrier to transfer electrons from the reducing agents to copper ions [2,3]. Colloid palladium catalyst will deposit on the surface of the substrate and initiate the electroless plating reaction during the pre-treatment step in electroless copper deposition. However, it remains a challenge to control the factors such as the deposition time, catalyst stability as well as the cost of the electroless copper deposition in the process. In PCB manufacturing process, Sn/Pd colloid is the most widely used activator, which is negatively charged stabilizing by the electrostatic interaction with its counter chloride ions around the stable layer of Sn2+ ions complex outside the palladium core [4,5]. However, Sn2+ ions are relatively easy to be oxidized to Sn4+, which makes the colloid easier to precipitate and lose its activity with time [6,7]. Moreover, additional accelerated steps are required to hydrolyze and remove the Sn2+ ion layer in the practical process. In addition, palladium is an extremely expensive metal, and the content of palladium catalyst directly determines the cost [8]. To this end, decrease of the content of palladium to lower the cost of the catalyst and adopting relatively simple operation process are highly required in industry. However, it is still a challenge to develop new activator and substitute strategy in the electroless copper plating process.

Bimetallic nanoparticles have attracted much attention because of their unique physical and chemical properties, as well as good catalytic activity and stability. The structure and composition of metal nanomaterials, and the colloidal protective agents are the key factors that can affect the activity of bimetal nanomaterials. In recent years, many bimetallic nanomaterials have been applied in electroless copper deposition, such as Cu-Pd, Ag-Pd, Pt-Pd, Au-Pd and Au-Cu [9–13]. However, it is still a challenge to develop a stable catalyst with simple synthesis, lower cost, and good catalytic activity in electroless copper deposition. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) is a common and effective colloidal protective agent and the carbonyl group in the pyrrolidone ring can interact with the metal surface to extract the metal density from the metal particles, thus affecting the electronic surface state [14]. Miguel-García's group synthesized Pd/PVP particles by solvent reduction method [15] and Chao Peng et al. utilized the synthesized Pd/PVP NPs in electroless copper plating [16]. Ag/Pd-PVP NPs were also synthesized and applied in the field of electroless copper plating, in which the PVP accelerated the reduction of silver and slowed down the reduction of Pd due to the steric resistance effect [17]. Though PVP is essential for the stability of Ag/Pd NPs, it also has the adverse effect on the catalytic activity. Therefore, how to prepare a stable bimetallic nanocatalyst as well as keep the catalytic activity is still the main challenge.

As one of the new kind of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, C-dots consist of graphite structure or amorphous carbon core and carbonaceous surfaces with rich oxygen-containing groups [18], showing great potential in detection, bioimaging, and photocatalysts [19]. The hydrophobic core and hydrophilic functional groups on the surface make C-dots amphiphilic properties [20], which can effectively prevent the agglomeration. Some C-dots can be acted as the reducing agents, resulting from the surface oxygen functionalities present on the carbon core of the system [20,21]. Via one-pot electrochemically carbonization of ethanediol [22,23], our group successfully synthesized the hydroxyl-rich C-dots which can reduce noble metal salts under mild and simple conditions to obtain metal NPs with good dispersity. The obtained metal NPs/C-dots composites can be utilized in optical detection, catalytic degradation, and electrochemical sensing [24–28]. However, the practical application of C-dots in industry is still in its infancy. Pd/C NCs obtained by reducing of Pd ions using C-dots as the reductant could catalyze the reduction of p-nitrophenol in the presence of sodium borohydride. However, it is interesting that Au/Pd nanocomposites (NCs) obtained by using C-dots as the reducing agents show improved catalytic performance in the reduction of p-nitrophenol than that of Pd/C NCs [29]. Inspired by this, it is supposed that the Pd contained bimetals/C-dots NCs may act as effective catalysts and play a key role in electroless copper plating. However, the actual particle size of the NCs, the content of Pd, and the effective catalytical sites need to be optimized due to the unstable agglomeration.

Herein, a kind of electroless copper plating catalyst with ultra-low palladium content was successfully synthesized by introducing another auxiliary metal salt and using C-dots as the reductants and protective agents together with PVP. Scheme 1 shows the synthesis of the catalyst and the application in the electroless copper deposition process. The Ag/C NCs were prepared using C-dots as the reducing agent, which were then acted as the templates for the synthesis of spherical bimetallic alloy AgPd/carbon nanocomposites (AgPd/C NCs) with silver-rich core and palladium-rich surface. The AgPd/C NCs were evenly dispersed in aqueous solution and effectively utilized in the electroless copper deposition. Through thoroughly characterization, it was found that the obtained copper layer has good adhesion, which was better than the traditional activator in industry. Under the condition of no loss of activity, the cost is successfully reduced, and the steps are simplified without the additional step for dissolving of the colloid. This provides a new idea for the synthesis of catalysts with good stability in electroless copper plating and greatly reduces the content of palladium without sacrificing the activity. It shows a good prospect to utilize the AgPd/C NCs as a substitute for Pd colloidal catalyst in the traditional process in the future.

HCl, HNO3, AgNO3, HAuCl4, ethanediol, hydroquinone and ascorbic acid were all analytical reagents and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). PdCl2 was bought from Shanghai Zhanyun Chemical Co., Ltd. NaOH was purchased from Tianjin Hengxing chemical reagent manufacture Co., Ltd. Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw 8000) was bought from Macklin (Shanghai, China). PCBs, swelling agent PA-7H, desmear PA-8, neutralizer PA-9X, alkaline cleaner PA-31, Presoak PA-33, colloid-Pd PA-34, accelerator PA-35 and copper plating liquid PA-6 were obtained from Hunan Litian Electronic Material Co., Ltd. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water (18 MΩ·cm, Millipore-Q).

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray mapping spectroscopy (EDS mapping) were conducted on JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) and edax energy spectrometer (Octane SDD, America), respectively. UV-vis data were obtained via UV-2600 (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted on Nexus-870 (Thermo Nicolet, America). X-Ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were recorded on the Rigaku 2500X-ray diffractometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by K-Alpha 1063 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, British). Optical microscope (OM), Eclipse Ti-S) were used to observe and evaluate the backlight level. The morphology of the copper film was characterized by JSM 7401 (JEOL, Japan) field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The deposition rate of the electroless copper was obtained gravimetrically using a balance (SHIMADZU AP135W). All the samples preparation is protected by inert gas and stored at vacuum. The contents of Cu, Pd and Ag dissolved by concentrated HNO3 were quantified by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS, Thermo Fisher ICAP RQ). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted on a research electrochemical workstation of SP-150 (Bio-logic Science Instruments, France), and the amplitude of 5 mV was applied for all EIS measurement. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was conducted on CHI 1030C electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua instrument, China). The electrochemical measurements were conducted in a conventional electrochemical cell with the glassy carbon electrode (GCE), saturated calomel electrode (SCE), and platinum wire used as the working electrode, reference electrode, and counter electrode, respectively. Zeta potential and particle size were tested on Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern, England).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ag/C NCs and Pd/C NCs
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots were electrochemically synthesized according to our previous work [23] and the process was shown in the Supporting information (S1). The Ag/C NCs were synthesized as follows: A certain volume of silver nitrate aqueous solution (10 mM) was added dropwise into 6 mL of the NaOH (10 mM)-treated C-dots aqueous solution and stirred for 3 h under 30 °C in water bath. The color of the solution changed from dark brown to light yellow, indicating the formation of Ag/C NCs. Ag/C NCs(1) and Ag/C NCs(2) were obtained with the addition of 397 μL AgNO3 and 277 μL AgNO3, respectively. The synthesis of Pd/C NCs was as follows: drop a certain volume of palladium hydrochloric acid solution (10 mM) into 5.5 mL of C-dots aqueous solution, stir for 1 h under the condition of 40 °C in water bath. Black agglomerated substances appeared, and the supernatant turned light yellow, indicating the successful synthesis of Pd/C NCs.

The AgPd/C NCs was synthesized as follows: Firstly, 0.27 g PVP was dissolved in 45 mL deionized water under stirring. Then Ag/C NCs aqueous solution and 10 mL ascorbic acid aqueous solution (20 g L−1) were added in turn. A certain amount of palladium chloride solution (10 mM) was added within 8 min at 45 °C, and the solution quickly turned brown, and then black, indicating the formation of Pd NPs. 25 mL deionized water was added under ultrasonic for 10 min, and then transferred to a 60 °C oil bath and mature for 1 h. Finally, the solution was transferred to a 40 °C water bath and added 10 mL of hydroquinone aqueous solution (10 g L−1), and the colloid catalyst was obtained after 20 min. Three kind of AgPd/C NCs materials were synthesized. AgPd/C NCs(1) with the palladium content of 10 ppm was synthesized by adding Ag/C NCs(1) and 940 μL palladium chloride solution; AgPd/C NCs(2) with the palladium content of 7 ppm was synthesized by adding Ag/C NCs(2) and 658 μL palladium chloride solution; Pd/C NCs(3) with palladium the content of 7 ppm was synthesized by adding C-dots solution and 658 μL palladium chloride.

To remove the copper coating and obtain clean surfaces, the PCBs were immersed in a mixture of H2SO4 and H2O2 for three days. PCBs were cut into 4 cm × 4 cm and the electroless copper plated process was conducted using the process described in Table S1 in the supporting information. The pre-leaching solution is C-dots solution, and the activation solution is the catalysts synthesized above. At the same time, the catalyst of Sn/Pd colloidal solution with the palladium concentration of 10 ppm and 7 ppm was also used for comparison. Multiple samples were simultaneously processed in the electroless copper baths, and the samples underwent different electroless deposition time were taken out, rinsed, and blow-dried with N2. The mass of each sample after electroless copper deposition was measured. And the deposited copper was then chemically stripped off from the samples by immersion the copper-covered PCBs in concentrated HNO3 for 1 min. Afterwards, the sample was rinsed with ultra-pure water, dried with N2, the mass was measured again. The difference between the two mass readings gave the amount of the deposited copper. After the electroless copper plating, the copper layers activated by AgPd/C NCs and Sn/Pd colloid were observed by optical microscopy (OM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of Ag/C NCs, Pd/C NCs and AgPd/C NCs
ce:section.ce:para: Ag/C NCs and Pd/C NCs were obtained by simple mixing of metal salts and C-dots, in which the C-dots acted as the reductant and protect agent. While AgPd/C NCs were synthesized using the Ag/C NCs as the template and C-dots as the reductant. The morphology of C-dots (A), Ag/C NCs (B), Pd/C NCs (C) and AgPd/C NCs (D) were investigated by TEM. Fig. 1 A shows that the C-dots are highly dispersed with the diameter smaller than 3 nm. The HRTEM image in Fig. 1F shows that the lattice spacing is 0.200 nm, which can be ascribed to the (102) diffraction plane of graphite carbon (sp2). As shown in Fig. 1B, the synthesized Ag/C NCs have good dispersion, and the shape is basically spherical with the size less than 10 nm. The lattice spacing of 0.235 nm is found in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1G), which corresponds to the (111) crystal plane of Ag NPs. Fig. 1C shows that the Pd/C nanoparticles with the diameter of 5–12 nm (Fig. S1D) gather into flowers structure surrounded by C-dots. The lattice spacing of 0.224 nm shown in the HRTEM image (Fig. 1H) can be attributed to the (111) diffraction plane of Pd. The synthesized AgPd/C NCs display as spherical structure with the size of 4-8 nm (Fig. S1C) as shown in Fig. 1D. In the HRTEM image (Fig. 1I), the lattice spacing of 0.321 nm and 0.284 nm appear in the core zone and in the edge zone, respectively, indicating that the alloy nanostructure formed with palladium rich surface and silver rich core. As Figs. 1C and 1D show, the intergranular distance distribution of AgPd/C NCs was more uniform compared with the synthesized Pd/C NCs. The EDX analysis in Fig. 1E verify that the AgPd/C NCs contain palladium and silver with good distribution, further indicating the formation of bimetallic NCs.

XPS was carried out to confirm the formation and chemical state of the prepared nanomaterials. Fig. 1J shows that Pd/C NCs is mainly composed of C, O and Pd, while AgPd/C NCs contain Ag, Pd, O and C. Fig. 1K shows that C1s has three different peaks at 284.68, 286.4 and 288.78 eV, attributed to the carbon species adsorbed on the surface of Pd NCs, C-O group and C=O group, respectively [25,29]. The XPS spectrum of Pd 3d (Fig. 1L) shows that there are two states of the chemical states of Pd on the surface of Pd/C NCs. The Pd 3d5/2 peak at 336.18 eV and Pd3d3/2 peak at 341.78 eV can be attributed to the metallic of Pd0. While the peaks shown at 337.98 eV and 343.18 eV originated from Pd2+, representing the unreacted palladium reagent [30,31]. The results showed that palladium NPs were successfully synthesized. In Fig. 1M, the high resolution XPS spectra of C1s of Pd/C NCs and AgPd/C NCs are basically the same. As shown in Fig. 1N, two peaks at 367.39 and 373.08 eV can be observed, which can be assigned to Ag 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively, agreed well with the theoretic value of the metallic Ag [32]. It demonstrates that the Ag (0) exists in the AgPd/C NCs. The Pd 3d spectrum of AgPd/C NCs could also be fitted into asymmetric peaks, suggesting the existence of two states of Pd species: The peaks of Pd 3d5/2 at 335.01 eV and Pd 3d3/2 at 340.28 eV attributed to the metallic Pd0, and the peaks at 336.58 eV and 338.88 eV originated from Pd2+. The results indicate that some oxide species may be produced on the surface of Pd in the process of drying [33]. Compared with Pd/C NCs, the binding energy of Pd 3d in AgPd/C NCs negatively shifted, indicating the electronic interaction between the Pd shell and the Ag core [34], which further illustrates the formation of the core-shell structure of AgPd/C NCs. As shown in Table S2, the measured contents ratio of Ag to Pd is far less than the actual added mass ratio of Ag to Pd in the preparation process. Owing to the detection depth of XPS is very shallow, only parts of Pd on the surface and parts of silver inside can be detected.

A broad diffraction peak appear at about 22° (2θ) in the XRD patterns of Pd/C NCs (Fig. 1P), suggesting the disorder carbon in Pd/C NCs [35]. Whereas Pd/C NCs do not show any obvious reflection peak of Pd because most of the surface of Pd/C NCs is covered by C-dots. The powder XRD spectrum of AgPd/C NCs (Fig. 1Q) shows five peaks at 39.64, 46.15, 67.70, 81.48 and 85.38°, which can be assigned to (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) of Pd, respectively. Meanwhile, it also shows a wide peak at 22°, indicating that the carbon also existed in the composite. However, independent Ag peaks are not obvious in the spectrum of AgPd/C NCs, which implies that most of the Ag were wrapped in the inner core [36].

The formation of NPs was further confirmed by UV spectra. In Fig. 2 A, a strong absorption peak at 263 nm can be found for the synthesized C-dots, which is attributed to the π-π* transition [37], while the absorption peak of Pd/C NCs occurs at 258 nm. And Ag/C NCs have a characteristic peak at 405 nm, consisting well with the typical plasma resonance absorption peak of Ag NCs. For the synthesized AgPd/C NCs, the characteristic peak of Ag disappeared, which indicates that Ag may be completely covered by Pd. As shown in the IR spectra in Fig. 2B, the characteristic peaks of hydroxyl (-OH) at 3415 cm−1 and carbonyl (C=O) at 1713 cm−1 of C-dots are observed. Compared with C-dots, the hydroxyl (-OH) intensity of Pd/C NCs and AgPd/C NCs decreased, and the carbonyl intensity increase relatively. These means that the -OH groups on the surface of C-dots play important roles as the reductant [21]. Fig. 2C shows that Pd/C NCs are mainly composed of palladium and carbon, while AgPd/C NCs mainly contain the elements of silver, palladium, and carbon (Fig. 2D). All these results suggest that core-shell AgPd bimetallic nanomaterials are prepared using Ag/C NCs as the templates and C-dots and PVP as reductant and colloidal protectants.

In addition, the stability of the obtained nanocomposite was studied. After a month of storage, the synthesized AgPd/C NCs and Pd/C NCs show completely different morphologies as shown in Fig. S1A and S1B. It displays that the AgPd/C NCs solution is in good dispersibility, and this may indicate that AgPd/C NCs can act as good catalyst. At the same time, the curing temperature plays an important role in the stabilization of the colloid. It is reported that Brownian motion is enhanced and more uniformly dispersed colloids with smaller particle size can be obtained with the increase of curing temperature and curing time. Here, the effect of curing temperature on particle size and dispersion were also investigated. As Fig. S2 in the supporting information shown, higher curing temperature and longer curing time will lead to the aggregation of the particles, which would increase the particles size and reduce the activity. Therefore, curing temperature of 60 °C and curing time of 1 h were used.

Therefore, the AgPd/C NCs were synthesized with palladium rich surface and silver rich core, which have superior stability, good dispersion, and possible high catalytic activity. And the material may be applied in electroless copper plating process in PCBs manufacturing process. And this will be investigated and discussed in the following study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity of Pd-based nanomaterials in electroless copper deposition
ce:section.ce:para: Because the activator is generally necessary to lower the activation energy of formation of metal by serving as a temporary electron bridge between the reducing agent and metallic ions [38], the choose of a good catalyst is the key to electroless copper deposition. As shown in Scheme 1, AgPd/C NCs were synthesized using the Ag/C NCs as the templates, and then were acted as the catalyst to activate the electroless copper plating experiments. The process of electroless copper deposition and conditions were stated in Table S1, which were just the same as the traditional ones in industry only replaced the activator by the AgPd/C NCs in the activation process. And at the same time, the acceleration process was omitted because the catalytic sites of the catalyst here were directly exposed without needing removal of the outer protective shell.

Four samples of AgPd/C NCs(1), AgPd/C NCs(2), Pd/C NCs(3), and Sn/Pd (Pd=7 ppm) colloidal catalyst were used in the electroless copper deposition experiment. The backlight rating scale is a parameter commonly used in industry to evaluate the coverage of copper. If the copper layer does not completely cover the entire surface of the hole wall or the copper layer is porous, light will pass through the defect points and the transparent points will be observed in the microscope. A perfectly dense layer of copper would appear entirely black with no bright spots, which means good coverage. As shown in Fig. S3, level 10 is used for fully covered copper, level 1 represents the worst case, and level 8 and above may be acceptable in the industry [16]. OM was used to observe the coverage of the obtained copper layer activated by these four catalysts. Fig. 3 A shows that the wall of the hole has been completely covered by copper film when using AgPd/C NCs(1) as the activator and the backlight coefficient reaches 9.5. Fig. 3B shows that when the palladium concentration in AgPd/C NCs drops to 7 ppm, there are 2 to 5 light transmission points on the hole wall after copper plating, and the backlight coefficient is 9. As shown in Fig. 3C, the light transmission point increases significantly using the Pd/C NCs(3) as the activator, and the backlight coefficient is about 8. In Fig. 3D, many light transmission points appear on the hole wall after Cu plating activated by the traditional Sn/Pd colloid with palladium concentration of 7 ppm, and the backlight coefficient is 7. These results show that the backlight performance of the Cu plating obtained by AgPd/C NCs activator is better than that of Sn/Pd activation under the same palladium concentration.

The morphology of copper films obtained using the activator of Sn/Pd colloid (Fig. 3E) and AgPd/C NCs (Fig. 3F) was observed by SEM. The surfaces of PCBs were almost completely covered by copper films in the presence of both activators. And the obtained Cu layer did not show porous morphology, which was different from those obtained from other bimetallic nanoparticles for copper plating as catalysts in literature [6,7]. Using the AgPd/C NCs catalyst as the activator, the structure of copper film was compact with smaller particles. This may indicate that the AgPd/C NCs have good adsorption ability on the surface of PCBs, which can facilitate the copper plating. The synthesized AgPd/C NCs is electronegative as Fig. 4 A shows, which is weaker than Sn/Pd colloid. Therefore, the adsorption force for the AgPd/C NCs on PCBs may be the electrostatic force and the van der Waals force.

Specific activity (defined as the amount of the deposited copper related to the amount of palladium per unit) is an effective parameter to evaluate the activity of the activator. It is generally considered that the chemical deposition rate is mainly affected by the activity of the activator during the induction time. Generally, the initial 2-4 min is the induction time, and the electroless copper plating transform into the process of copper deposition on the copper film, and the activator properties may gradually lose its influence in the latter process. To exclude any systematic and contribution from co-deposited reagents, such as the catalyst of AgPd/C NCs, since the catalyst might adsorb on the PCBs in the activated process, we tested the amount of the copper, the adsorbed palladium and silver at different copper deposition times using the ICP-MS. The tested solution was diluted 100 times, and the results are shown in Table S3. Compared with the deposition amount of copper, the adsorption amounts of palladium and silver are basically negligible. Since the activation conditions are the same, the adsorption amount of palladium on PCBs with different copper deposition times is basically similar. Therefore, the catalyst of AgPd/C NCs has little influence on the copper deposition rate. And the rate of electroless copper plating using AgPd/C NCs(2) as the activator can be directly measured by gravimetric method. The measurements were conducted for 3 times to obtain the average mass of the deposited copper, and the error bars represent the 95% confidence interval, and the results are shown in Fig. 4B. The mass of the deposited copper almost changes linearly with time, which implies a constant deposition rate in this process. The specific activity of AgPd/C NCs(2) in the first 2 min of induction was comparable to that of commercial Sn/Pd colloids [16,39]. This indicates that the active sites of the catalysts are effective and exposed. The average slope represents the overall rate for the deposition of Cu [39], which can be calculated as 5.7 × 10−4 mg cm−2 s−1, and the thickness of copper film obtained by electroless copper plating for 10 min at this speed is greater than 15 u" (1 μm=39.398 u"), which indicates that the electroless copper plating rate activated by AgPd/C NCs reaches the industrial standard.

In the electroless plating of metal precipitation, the catalyst acts as the carrier for the transfer of electrons from reducing agent to metal ions. The smaller the resistance of the catalyst indicates the effect of the catalyst as the carrier. To further confirm the main mechanism and the possible reason for the good performance of electroless metal precipitation, EIS experiments were conducted, as shown in Fig. 4C. Compared to C-dots and Ag/C NCs, the charge transfer resistance of AgPd/C NCs decreased, indicating the good conductivity of AgPd/C NCs. Therefore, the good catalytic performance of the AgPd/C NCs in the electroless copper deposition may firstly originate from the good conductivity of the catalyst.

The oxidative and reductive activity of Pd-contained NCs for electroless copper deposition can be analyzed by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) [40]. The half-cell reactions of cupric ion reduction, formaldehyde oxidation, and the overall redox reaction are shown as follows:
Cu2+ + 2 e− → Cu
2 HCHO + 4 OH− → 2 HCOO−+2 H2O + H2 + 2 e−
Cu2+ + 2 HCHO + 4 OH− → Cu + 2 HCOO− + 2 H2O + H2

Fig. 5 A plots the current-potential curves of copper ion reduction in the cathodic bath, whose components are shown in Table S4 in the supporting information. As the palladium concentration of AgPd/C NCs increased from 7 ppm to 10 ppm, the reduction potential moved from -0.585 V to -0.572 V [41], and the current density increased from 0.441 mA cm−2 to 0.642 mA cm−2. For comparison, Sn/Pd colloid with the palladium concentration of 7 ppm and 10 ppm were also investigated, and the reduction potential was found changed from -0.601 V to -0.597 V, and the current density increased from 0.285 mA cm−2 to 0.304 mA cm−2. The positive shifts of the reductive potential and the increased current both indicate the good catalytic activity of the catalyst. Therefore, these results meant that the deposition rate of electroless copper is accelerated with the increase of Pd content, which also agree with the literature [42–44]. At the same palladium concentration, the peak potential for the reduction is more positive with enhanced peak current in the case of AgPd/C NCs than that of Sn/Pd colloid. For the anodic reaction (Fig. 5B), with the increase of palladium concentration, the peak potential for oxidation do not change significantly, however, the peak current change greatly. The limiting diffusion current densities of AgPd/C NCs(1) and AgPd/C NCs(2) are 0.617 mA cm−2 and 0.197 mA cm−2, respectively. The limiting diffusion current densities of Sn/Pd (Pd=10 ppm) colloid and Sn/Pd (Pd=7 ppm) colloid are 0.076 mA cm−2 and 0.053 mA cm−2. The oxidation current of AgPd/C NCs is much higher than that of Sn/Pd colloid and the main reason may result from the better conductivity of AgPd/C NCs than that of Sn/Pd colloid. After AgPd/C NCs(1) and AgPd/C NCs(2) were stored for one month, the anodic polarization curve was measured again, as shown in Fig. 5C. It is found that the peak for the oxidation current density decrease to 0.076 mA cm−2 and 0.074 mA cm−2, which is better than that of the current density of the newly prepared Sn/Pd colloid. And the decrease of the oxidation current density of AgPd/C NCs(2) is not obvious, indicating that the activity of the catalyst can still last for a long time.

Mixed potential theory (MPT), which is described by Tafel equation, is commonly utilized to analyze the rate of the electroless metal deposition. According to the MPT, the coordinates of the intersection point between the anodic and cathodic curves yields the deposition current (IMPT) and the mixed potential (EMPT) at which the deposition reaction is in the steady state. Fig. 5D plots the electrochemical current-potential curves of the Tafel equation of electroless copper deposition processes catalyzed by AgPd/C NCs(1) and AgPd/C NCs(2) gave the current densities at the intersections of 2.51 mA cm−2 and 0.75 mA cm−2, respectively. The electroless copper deposition rate (RMPT) can be obtained by MPT given by Faraday's law [45] with the equation of RMPT= I MPT × 1.18 mg h−1·cm−2 and the RMPT can be calculated as 8.3 × 10−4 mg cm−2 and 2.5 × 10−4 mg cm−2 on the basis of AgPd/C NCs(1) and AgPd/C NCs(2). The results are close to the copper deposition rate calculated by gravimetric method and strongly supports the AgPd/C NCs catalyst as a potential activator in chemical deposition with high quality. Therefore, compared with Sn/Pd colloidal catalyst, AgPd/C NCs catalyst has excellent catalytic activity with lower palladium content, which shows great potential electroless copper deposition in industry.

Carbon-based AgPd NCs with palladium-rich surface and silver-rich core were synthesized using C-dots as the reductant and protective agents. After optimization, the AgPd/C NCs were used as catalysts for electroless copper plating during the activation process. Compared with the traditional electroless copper plating process using Sn/Pd colloid as the catalyst, this method completely breaks the idea of using SnCl2 as the reductant and stabilize agent, which can avoid the instability of the colloid caused by oxidation of Sn2+ ions. Via replacing part of palladium with auxiliary Ag, the concentration of palladium greatly reduced lower to 7 ppm with higher copper precipitation rate and the specific activity can be comparable to traditional Sn/Pd colloidal activator. Meanwhile, the dispersion and stability of AgPd/C NCs are better than those of Sn/Pd colloidal activator. It also provides a new idea for electroless copper plating process, which is expected to replace the traditional Sn/Pd colloid activator, showing great prospects in the future.

Luo Luo: Conceptualization, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Peipei Li: Validation, Writing – review & editing. Xiang Liu: Formal analysis, Validation. Wensheng Zeng: Investigation. Youyu Zhang: Investigation, Methodology. Meiling Liu: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Shouzhuo Yao: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21974042, 21904039, 21645008), the Scientific Research Fund of Hunan Provincial Education Department (18A010), the Science and Technology Department of Hunan Province (2021JJ30012).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.141679.
